A system is proposed to improve itself by using its current outputs to evaluate and shape its next outputs. The proposal depends on a threshold: below it, outputs are too unreliable to be useful judges; above it, they become reliable enough to guide further improvement. But no order parameter is given for this threshold. So in the thought experiment, there is no measurable quantity that cleanly marks the transition. People may still speak as if a phase change occurs, but they cannot specify what variable crosses what boundary.

For recursive self-improvement to work, the evaluator must be more stable than the generator it is correcting. Otherwise, error in generation is merely fed back through an equally unstable judge. In this world, that stability is asserted as plausible but not explained. So the loop can be imagined, but not grounded. The system may generate outputs and score them, yet there is no justified reason to expect the scoring process to preserve or improve truth better than generation alone.

Now add the stack of mediation. Outputs are represented, then evaluated through further representations, then used to condition future outputs. At each layer, apparent structure may arise from how things are encoded rather than from contact with anything newly discovered. In such a loop, “novelty” can appear simply because the representational scheme highlights some distinctions and suppresses others. The system may seem to uncover new patterns while only reorganizing its own artifacts.

The final two premises introduce terms without explained role: “Understrapper assoiling unbethink existentialistically spogel” and “Mangel wielding brucins seigniorage swede.” Taken as true, they add facts or sentences to the world, but without defined connection to the threshold, the evaluator, the generator, or the mediation stack. Since they are not linked by any stated relation, they cannot repair the missing order parameter, cannot establish evaluator stability, and cannot distinguish discovery from representational artifact. They remain true but inert with respect to the main mechanism.

So the resulting world is one where a recursive improvement scheme is imaginable but underdetermined. A threshold is claimed without a criterion. A self-judging loop is proposed without showing the judge is steadier than what it judges. A multilayer mediation process makes it impossible to tell whether apparent innovation is real or merely a consequence of internal representation. Additional uninterpreted truths exist, but they do not alter those limitations. The system may continue producing and rating outputs, and observers may narrate this as improvement, but the premises do not entitle anyone to conclude that genuine discovery or reliable self-improvement is occurring.
